topics.title: Abbasid Caliphate
topics.region: Iraq, Iran, Syria and wider Islamic world
topics.summary: A major caliphal dynasty associated with Baghdad, translation activity, urban culture and long-running political change.
topics.story: The interesting part is not one golden palace scene but the movement of books, scholars, paper, merchants and officials through Baghdad. The page should point back to chronicles and modern scholarship before any cinematic narration.

topics.title: Ancient Egypt
topics.summary: A long civilization of dynasties, temples, writing, administration and royal ideology.
topics.story: A fun source-based entry can follow a scribe, a river flood, a temple workshop and a royal inscription while clearly separating archaeology from later legends.

topics.title: Mesopotamia and Gilgamesh
topics.summary: Cities, cuneiform tablets, kingship traditions and epic literature from Sumer, Akkad, Babylonia and Assyria.
topics.story: Gilgamesh belongs in a legends-and-literature lane: fascinating, ancient and culturally important, but not presented as simple factual biography.

topics.title: Troy and the Homeric epics
topics.era: Bronze Age setting / later Greek epic tradition
topics.summary: A mix of archaeology, oral poetry and later literary memory around Troy, Achilles, Hector and Odysseus.
topics.story: Readers should see how archaeology, epic poetry and later imagination differ, then enjoy the story as a cultural tradition.

topics.title: Alexander the Great
topics.region: Macedon, Persia, Egypt, Central Asia and India
topics.summary: A Macedonian king whose campaigns connected the eastern Mediterranean with the Persian imperial world and beyond.
topics.story: His life is full of dramatic episodes—crossing into Asia, founding cities, entering Egypt, reaching India—best told with Plutarch, Arrian and modern notes separated.

topics.title: Ashoka and Mauryan India
topics.summary: Mauryan imperial power and Ashoka's inscriptions are central to ancient Indian history.
topics.story: An engaging story can move from conquest and remorse to inscriptions carved across landscapes, but must distinguish inscriptional evidence from later tradition.

topics.title: Han Dynasty China
topics.summary: A major imperial period associated with administration, frontiers, histories and Silk Road links.
topics.story: The fun angle follows envoys, court historians, frontier garrisons and trade routes while pointing to Chinese historical records.

topics.title: Mughal Empire
topics.summary: A Persianate empire in South Asia known for administration, art, architecture, memoirs and court chronicles.
topics.story: Babur's memoirs make the opening feel personal: gardens, battles, losses and observations, clearly marked as source-derived reading.

personalities.name: Ibn Battuta
personalities.region: Morocco to India, China and beyond
personalities.story: A source-based story mode can follow his sea voyages, court appointments, dangerous roads and surprises in unfamiliar cities while linking back to the Rihla.

personalities.name: Ibn Khaldun
personalities.story: His great personality story is intellectual adventure: courts rise and fall, tribes and cities change, and he turns lived politics into historical theory.

personalities.name: Al-Biruni
personalities.story: A fascinating route through languages, astronomy, measurement and cross-cultural study; ideal for a verified scientist profile.

personalities.name: Al-Khwarizmi
personalities.story: A story can connect calculation, maps, astronomy and Baghdad's scholarly networks without exaggerating beyond sources.

personalities.name: Salahuddin Ayyubi
personalities.story: The compelling narrative is leadership, coalition-building, Jerusalem and reputation across Muslim and European sources.

personalities.name: Harun al-Rashid
personalities.story: Separate court history from later legendary tales: both are interesting, but the library must label which is chronicle and which is literary memory.

personalities.name: Hatshepsut
personalities.story: Her story combines kingship, monuments, trade expeditions and later attempts to reshape memory through inscriptions and images.

personalities.name: Hannibal Barca
personalities.story: The famous Alpine crossing is exciting, but the page should ground it in Polybius/Livy traditions and modern historical analysis.

personalities.name: Cleopatra VII
personalities.story: A powerful personality page can separate Roman propaganda, later legend and political history in the age of Caesar and Antony.

personalities.name: Zheng He
personalities.story: His voyages make a great map-based story with ports, diplomacy, ships and source-linked routes.

legends.title: Gilgamesh and the search for life
legends.note: Legend/literature, not treated as straightforward factual history.
legends.hook: A king, a friend, a forest journey and a question humans still ask: what remains after a life of power?

legends.title: The Trojan War cycle
legends.note: Epic tradition with archaeological questions kept separate.
legends.hook: Achilles, Hector, Odysseus and Helen belong in a literature-and-memory room linked to ancient texts and archaeology.

legends.title: Rama's exile and return
legends.note: Sacred/literary tradition; present respectfully and label genre clearly.
legends.hook: A journey through exile, loyalty, conflict and return that shaped art, performance and moral imagination across regions.

legends.title: Rustam and the Shahnameh heroes
legends.note: Epic/literary tradition; not presented as simple political chronicle.
legends.hook: Heroic battles, family tragedy and kingship ideals from the Persianate literary world.

legends.title: Arabian Nights story worlds
legends.note: Literary story tradition; useful for culture, not as direct factual chronicle.
legends.hook: Merchants, sailors, cities and wonders show how medieval story collections imagined a connected world.
